name: Analytic rubric
summary: Matrix that assesses separate criteria using described levels.
desc:
The analytic rubric is a matrix that breaks the task down into separate criteria and describes the performance levels for each. Each criterion is assessed independently, allowing precise feedback on which aspects are strong and which need improvement.

How to use it: design a table where rows are the criteria (e.g., 'structure', 'argumentation', 'linguistic accuracy') and columns are the levels (e.g., 'excellent', 'satisfactory', 'developing', 'beginning'). Each cell contains a descriptor explaining what performance at that level looks like for that criterion. When assessing, the teacher reads each row and marks the level that best describes what was observed.

It is the most suitable assessment tool for complex tasks where detailed feedback matters. It requires design time, but once created it can be reused and shared with students as a guide.
purpose: Assessing complex tasks with multiple aspects.
advantages: Clarifies expectations and allows precise feedback.
limitations: Requires design time and good descriptors.

name: Global or holistic rubric
summary: Rubric that assesses performance holistically.
desc:
The holistic rubric assesses performance globally, offering a description of each level that covers the task as a whole without separating individual criteria. The assessor selects the level that best describes the work in its entirety.

How to use it: write 3–5 global descriptions ordered from highest to lowest quality. When assessing, read the complete work and select the description that best fits. It is faster than the analytic rubric but offers less diagnostic information.

Suitable when time is limited, when the task is short, or when a quick overall assessment is needed. For work where detailed feedback is a priority, the analytic rubric is more appropriate.
purpose: Assessing a complete task without separating all criteria.
limitations: Offers less diagnostic information.

name: Process rubric
summary: Rubric focused on planning, monitoring, revision and improvement.
desc:
The process rubric focuses assessment on how the task is developed: planning, organisation, revisions, time management, decision-making and use of resources. It does not assess the final product, but the path taken to reach it.

How to use it: criteria include aspects such as 'defines clear objectives at the outset', 'records progress and difficulties', 'reviews and adjusts the plan when necessary' or 'manages the group's time well'. It is applied during the development of the project, not at the end.

It is essential in long-duration projects, research and learning portfolios, where the learning process is as valuable as the result. It enables difficulties to be identified early and feedback to be given while there is still time to act.
purpose: Assessing how a task is developed.
advantages: Makes the process visible, not just the result.
limitations: Requires ongoing monitoring.

name: Product rubric
summary: Rubric focused on the quality of the final product.
desc:
The product rubric evaluates the characteristics of the final artefact submitted: quality, structure, content, presentation, coherence and compliance with requirements. Unlike the process rubric, it does not consider how the result was achieved.

How to use it: criteria focus on the product itself: 'the information is accurate and relevant', 'the structure is clear and coherent', 'the format is appropriate for the audience'. Each criterion has described levels that allow the work to be located.

It complements the process rubric in extended projects. Used alone, it may lead to assessing only the result and not the learning.
suitable_for: Written, digital, visual or physical products.
advantages: Allows quality to be assessed with clear criteria.
limitations: May leave the process out if used alone.

name: Oral rubric
summary: Rubric for presentations, debates, defences or interviews.
desc:
The oral rubric is an analytic rubric designed specifically to assess oral performances: presentations, debates, project defences, talks, interviews or colloquia.

How to use it: common criteria include 'clarity and organisation of the discourse', 'use of subject-specific vocabulary', 'eye contact and posture', 'response to questions' and 'adherence to time limits'. During the performance, the assessor marks the level of each criterion while observing. If there are many students, the performance can be recorded for later review (with the necessary permissions).

Sharing it before the performance allows students to prepare more effectively and reduces anxiety by knowing exactly what will be assessed.
purpose: Assessing oral communication, content and interaction.
advantages: Facilitates observation of diverse aspects of oral communication.
limitations: Can be difficult to record everything live.

name: Cooperative work rubric
summary: Rubric for assessing collaboration and group functioning.
desc:
The cooperative work rubric assesses the quality of collaboration within the group: equitable participation, communication, individual responsibility, conflict resolution and contribution to the shared goal.

How to use it: it can be applied by the teacher through observation, by the student themselves through self-assessment, or by peers through peer assessment (or all three at once). Criteria should distinguish between observable behaviours ('participates actively in discussions', 'completes assigned tasks') and group outcomes.

It is especially useful for preventing group work from diluting individual responsibility and for giving feedback on social and collaborative skills.
purpose: Assessing contribution, roles, responsibility and communication.
limitations: Requires evidence of real participation.

name: Competency rubric
summary: Matrix linked to criteria and competency performance levels.
desc:
The competency rubric is designed to assess the degree of development of a specific competency (communicative, mathematical, digital, scientific, etc.) through a contextualised task. Its criteria are aligned with the operational descriptors of the educational level.

How to use it: criteria reflect the key elements of the competency (e.g., for the communicative competency: 'understands complex texts', 'produces texts appropriate to the context', 'argues with evidence'). Each criterion has 3–4 described levels. It can be used to assess a single task or as a cumulative profile over the course.

It is the most suitable assessment tool for competency-based assessment required by the current curricular framework.
purpose: Assessing the mobilisation of knowledge in context.
advantages: Aligns assessment with criteria and competencies.
limitations: Must formulate observable indicators.

name: Checklist
summary: List of indicators with presence or absence.
desc:
The checklist is an assessment tool that lists the elements, steps or behaviours that must be present in a task or performance. For each item, the assessor simply marks whether it is present (yes/no) or has been fulfilled.

How to use it: draw up a list of concrete, observable indicators (e.g., 'includes an introduction', 'cites sources', 'uses safety equipment'). During or after the activity, mark each item. There are no intermediate levels; it is a binary check.

Fast to apply and very useful for activities with clear, closed requirements: laboratory protocols, presentations with defined requirements, technical procedures. For tasks with qualitative nuances, a rubric or scale is preferable.
purpose: Checking requirements, steps or specific evidence.
advantages: Fast, clear and easy to use.
limitations: Does not assess quality or nuance well.

name: Observation scale
summary: Scale for assessing frequency, intensity or degree of a performance.
desc:
The observation scale records the degree to which a behaviour, attitude or skill is displayed during an activity. Unlike the checklist (yes/no), it offers several points on the scale (e.g., always / almost always / sometimes / rarely / never).

How to use it: define the indicators to be observed and choose a frequency or quality scale. During the activity, the teacher marks the observed level for each student. It can be designed to observe several students at once if the indicators are few and specific.

Useful for assessing participation, attitudes, procedures and social skills in group activities, debates, practical activities and workshops.
suitable_for: Participation, cooperative work, practical activities, presentations.
limitations: Can be ambiguous without clear descriptors.

name: Rating scale
summary: Graduated scale for assessing degree of achievement.
desc:
The rating scale assigns a numerical or qualitative value to each criterion assessed, without reaching the precision of a rubric in describing what each level means. It is an intermediate point between the checklist and the analytic rubric.

How to use it: list the criteria and place a scale next to each one (e.g., 1–4 or insufficient/sufficient/good/excellent). The assessor assigns a value to each criterion based on their judgement. It is faster than a rubric because it does not require detailed descriptors for each cell.

Suitable when the teacher has enough experience to apply criteria implicitly, but may be less reliable and less informative for students than a rubric with descriptors.
suitable_for: Tasks of low or medium complexity.
limitations: Less precise than a full rubric.

name: Descriptive scale
summary: Scale whose levels include descriptions.
desc:
The descriptive scale is a variant of the rating scale in which each point on the scale is accompanied by a verbal description explaining what characterises that level. It is more precise than the numerical scale but less detailed than the analytic rubric.

How to use it: for each criterion, write 3–4 descriptions ordered from highest to lowest quality. When assessing, read the description that best corresponds to the observed work. It can be combined with a numerical score associated with each level.

It is a good starting point for teachers who want to introduce more explicit criteria without investing the time required to design a full analytic rubric.
purpose: Assessing quality with greater clarity than a numerical scale.
limitations: Requires writing descriptors.

name: Correction guide
summary: Document with criteria, expected answers or marking guidelines.
desc:
The correction guide specifies what should be found in a correct or quality response: which concepts, which arguments, which steps, which elements. It guides the marking of written, oral or practical tests.

How to use it: before marking, the teacher draws up a guide listing the expected points in each question or task, indicating how many marks each is worth. When marking, the student's response is compared with the guide and the corresponding score is assigned. It can include examples of acceptable responses or common errors.

It increases the consistency of marking across different marking sessions by the same teacher or across different teachers marking the same exam.
purpose: Marking tests or tasks with common criteria.
limitations: Less suitable for very open tasks if not adapted.

name: Correction template
summary: Format for recording scores or correct answers.
desc:
The correction template is a structured document that organises the marking task: it lists the questions or tasks, indicates the maximum score for each, and provides space to record the score obtained and observations.

How to use it: it is prepared before marking and used as a recording sheet during the process. It can include model answers, general criteria and notes on typical errors detected during marking.

Especially useful when there are many tests to mark or when several teachers are marking the same instrument, as it guarantees uniformity and speeds up the process.
limitations: Can reduce assessment to correct answers.

name: Scoring scale
summary: Distribution of points among sections or indicators.
desc:
The scoring scale is a scoring system that establishes how much each part of a test, task or set of criteria is worth, and how partial scores are translated into an overall mark.

How to use it: define the maximum score for each question, exercise or criterion, and indicate the formula for calculating the final mark (direct sum, weighting, rounding). If there is a penalty for errors, it must be clearly specified. The scoring scale should be made public before the test so that students know how much each part is worth.

A well-designed scoring scale reflects the relative importance of each content area or competency assessed and facilitates objective grading.
suitable_for: Tests and tasks with defined scoring.
advantages: Makes the distribution of marks explicit.
limitations: Can become mechanical without qualitative criteria.

name: Anecdotal record
summary: Brief note of significant observed events.
desc:
The anecdotal record is a document where the teacher briefly and with dates notes significant events observed in students: notable progress, a persistent difficulty, outstanding behaviour, a relevant contribution.

How to use it: it can be a notebook, a card per student, an app or a sheet divided by student. The key is to record the event as observed (without interpreting it yet) and add the date. Over time, the accumulation of anecdotes reveals patterns of learning or behaviour that would otherwise go unnoticed.

Especially useful in stages without numerical grading, for completing descriptive reports, guiding tutorials and grounding continuous formative assessment.
purpose: Collecting relevant evidence not always anticipated.
limitations: Not sufficient on its own for grading.

name: Descriptive record
summary: Detailed record of performances, processes or behaviours.
desc:
The descriptive record is a narrative assessment instrument in which the teacher describes in some detail the performance of a student or group during an activity or period, without reducing it to a score.

How to use it: write a qualitative description explaining what the student did, how they did it and what evidence was observed. It can be structured by criteria or by moments in the activity. It is more elaborate than the anecdotal record and more appropriate for periodic reports.

It allows families and the student themselves to receive a rich and nuanced picture of learning, beyond what a numerical grade can convey.
advantages: Allows the context of the evidence to be understood.
limitations: Time-consuming.

name: Teacher's diary
summary: Notebook or document with observations and pedagogical decisions.
desc:
The teacher's diary is a notebook or digital document where the teacher records reflections on how lessons develop: what worked, what did not, what students learned, what difficulties arose and what would be changed next time.

How to use it: it is worth writing in it regularly (at the end of each session or week) and reflectively, not just descriptively. Questions such as 'What learning evidence did I observe today?', 'Which students concern me and why?' or 'What would I adjust?' guide the reflection.

It is not just a record of what happened, but a tool for improving teaching practice. Over time, it becomes a valuable source of information for adjusting planning.
limitations: Can be unsystematic if not structured.

name: Individual tracking sheet
summary: Record of a student's progress over time.
desc:
The individual tracking sheet gathers, for each student, cumulative information about their development over a period: tasks submitted, results, observations, attendance at tutorials, improvement commitments.

How to use it: design a card per student with the criteria or indicators to be monitored. The teacher updates it periodically with information from different sources. At the end of the period, it offers a longitudinal view of progress.

Especially useful for personalised monitoring in groups with diverse needs, for preparing individual tutorials and for grounding promotion decisions.
limitations: Requires constant updating.

name: Group tracking sheet
summary: Record of a group's progress and functioning.
desc:
The group tracking sheet is a collective version of the individual tracking sheet: it gathers in one table information on all students in the group, making it possible to compare and detect general trends.

How to use it: each row is a student and each column is a criterion, task or moment of observation. The teacher updates it periodically. A glance at the whole reveals which students need more attention, which criteria the group in general has not reached and which aspects have improved.

It is a very useful classroom monitoring tool for teachers with multiple groups, as it maintains a global view of the state of learning.
limitations: Can hide individual differences if used alone.

name: Participation record
summary: Note of contributions and quality of input.
desc:
The participation record documents students' contributions during sessions: who participates, how often, what quality the contributions have and whether they respect turn-taking and participation rules.

How to use it: it can be as simple as a student list where each contribution is marked, or as elaborate as an observation scale assessing the quality of contributions. The key is that it is manageable during class and does not distract the teacher from their main role.

It allows objective feedback to be given on participation, identifies who never contributes and grounds this element if it forms part of the grade.
advantages: Allows participation to be made objective.
limitations: Quantity must not be confused with quality.

name: Cooperative work record
summary: Document for noting roles, contributions and group compliance.
desc:
The cooperative work record documents how the group functions during collaborative activities: distribution of roles, completion of individual tasks, quality of communication and conflict resolution.

How to use it: it can be combined with direct observation by the teacher, group self-assessment and peer assessment among members. Recording what happens in each group during the session allows timely intervention if a group is having difficulties.

Especially useful in extended group projects and activities, where the team's functioning carries as much weight as the final result.
limitations: Needs observation or complementary evidence.

name: Field notes
summary: Teacher's notes during activities, practical sessions or field trips.
desc:
Field notes are brief, quick and informal notes taken by the teacher during class to avoid losing learning evidence that would otherwise be forgotten. They are the raw material that can later be elaborated into more formal records.

How to use them: they can be taken on paper, a phone or a tablet. The key is that they are immediate, specific and dated. They do not need to be complete or formal; the aim is to capture the essentials for later retrieval.

They are the basis of incidental observation and anecdotal records. Over time, accumulated field notes allow the progress of each student to be seen with more precision than the teacher's memory.
limitations: Require subsequent organisation.

name: Assessment card
summary: Brief document for assessing a specific task.
desc:
The assessment card is a structured document that gathers the assessment of a student or piece of work according to predefined criteria, with space for scores, levels and qualitative comments.

How to use it: design it with the criteria relevant to the task, the rating scale and space for observations. It can be given to the student after marking as a feedback document. If used for all submissions, the student can see their progress throughout the course.

More flexible than a fixed rubric and more informative than a grade alone. Suitable for periodic assessments of individual work.
suitable_for: Tasks of low or medium complexity.
limitations: Less complete than a rubric.

name: Feedback card
summary: Template for returning strengths and areas for improvement.
desc:
The feedback card is a document designed specifically to communicate to the student what they have done well, what they can improve and how to do it. It goes beyond a score and focuses on actionable information.

How to use it: a common structure includes: 'What have you done well?', 'What can you improve?' and 'How can you improve it?'. The 'two stars and a wish' format or a table with strengths and areas for improvement can also be used. It is given together with or instead of the grade, especially in formative assessment.

Feedback is most effective when it is specific, timely and improvement-oriented. A well-designed card helps the teacher be systematic in this process.
limitations: Does not always produce a grade.

name: Assessment matrix
summary: Table relating criteria, indicators and levels.
desc:
The assessment matrix is a table that crosses assessment criteria with achievement levels, similar to a rubric but often more concise. It can include qualitative descriptors, scores or both.

How to use it: design it with criteria in rows and levels in columns. Each cell can contain a description, a score or both. When assessing, mark the cell corresponding to the level observed for each criterion.

It is a generic term that encompasses analytic rubrics. The main difference from a rubric is that the matrix can be more schematic and does not always include complete descriptors in each cell.
limitations: Can be complex for students.

name: Descriptor bank
summary: Collection of phrases for describing performance levels.
desc:
The descriptor bank is a collection of descriptive phrases ready to use in constructing rubrics, reports and feedback cards. It avoids having to write descriptors from scratch each time.

How to use it: it is organised by criteria, competencies or areas, with descriptors for different performance levels. When designing a rubric, the teacher selects and adapts descriptors from the bank instead of writing them from scratch.

It is an efficiency tool that reduces the time needed to design assessment instruments and promotes terminological coherence across different subjects or teachers in the same department.
limitations: Must be adapted to the specific criterion.

name: Achievement profile
summary: Representation of the degree achieved in criteria or competencies.
desc:
The achievement profile describes, for each competency or assessment criterion, what the student can do at a given moment and what level they are at. It is a snapshot of the current state of learning.

How to use it: it is drawn up from information gathered with different instruments over the course of the process. It can be presented as a table, a radar chart or a descriptive text. It is used to inform families and students, guide tutorials and plan support.

Within the framework of competency-based assessment, the achievement profile is the reference for grading each specific competency.
advantages: Offers a global view of learning.
limitations: Requires several prior data points.

name: Assessment target
summary: Radial representation of several assessed aspects.
desc:
The assessment target is a circular graphic divided into sectors, each corresponding to a criterion. Students (or the teacher) shade each sector from the centre outward to the level they consider achieved, creating a visual image of the performance profile.

How to use it: print or draw a target with as many sectors as criteria to be assessed. Each sector is divided into 3–5 concentric rings representing the levels. Each sector is coloured up to the level achieved. The result is an easily interpreted graphic at a glance.

Especially useful for self-assessment, peer assessment and visual communication of progress. Its graphic format makes it very accessible for students of any age.
limitations: Less precise than a rubric.

name: Assessment traffic light
summary: Visual system of achievement, doubt or difficulty.
desc:
The assessment traffic light uses the colours red, yellow and green to indicate the level of achievement of each criterion or task: green (achieved), yellow (developing) and red (not achieved or beginning).

How to use it: apply it to a list of criteria, tasks or indicators. The teacher or the student assigns a colour to each item according to the level reached. It is immediately readable and very visual, making it easy to identify at a glance where each student stands.

Suitable for quick formative monitoring, communication to families and self-assessment. It is complemented by written feedback explaining what to do to move from red to green.
advantages: Very quick and easy to understand.
limitations: Low precision.

name: Self-assessment scale
summary: Scale for students to assess their own achievement.
desc:
The self-assessment scale is an assessment instrument that the student uses to evaluate their own performance, process or product according to predefined criteria. It can be numerical, frequency-based or qualitative.

How to use it: provide the student with a list of indicators and a scale for each. The student completes it individually and honestly. For it to be effective, the criteria must have been worked on beforehand and the student must understand what each level means.

Its value is primarily metacognitive: it requires the student to reflect on their own learning. It can be combined with the teacher's assessment to identify discrepancies and address them in tutoring.
limitations: Can be biased without criteria.

name: Self-assessment rubric
summary: Rubric used by students on their own work.
desc:
The self-assessment rubric is an analytic rubric that the student applies to their own work or performance. It has the same structure as any rubric (criteria + described levels), but is written in the first person or in accessible language for the student.

How to use it: the student reads the descriptors for each criterion and selects the level that best describes their own performance. It is useful to also add evidence or justification for their choice. Comparing the self-assessment with the teacher's is an activity of great formative value.

Especially useful in learning portfolios, projects and complex tasks where the student can review their own work calmly.
purpose: Assessing one's own performance with criteria.
limitations: Requires training.

name: Peer assessment rubric
summary: Rubric for assessing peers' work.
desc:
The peer assessment rubric is an analytic rubric that students apply to the work or performance of their peers. It allows the peer review to be structured with clear criteria and described levels.

How to use it: each student receives the rubric and applies it to a peer's work (assigned randomly or on rotation). It can be done anonymously to reduce bias. Qualitative comments are as important as the assigned score.

For it to be effective, students must have practised applying the criteria before using it in peer assessment. The teacher should review the results and use them as a starting point for discussion.
limitations: Can involve personal bias.

name: Peer assessment card
summary: Brief template for assessing peers.
desc:
The peer assessment card is a simplified version of the peer assessment rubric: it usually includes fewer criteria, more direct questions and space for free comments. It is designed to make the peer review process more accessible and quick.

How to use it: it includes questions such as 'What has your peer done well?', 'What could they improve?' and 'What score would you give them on this criterion and why?'. It is given to the assessed student along with the returned work.

It is a good introduction to peer assessment for groups with no prior experience, as its format is less demanding than a full rubric.
limitations: Less detailed than a rubric.

name: Self-assessment checklist
summary: Checklist used by students to review requirements.
desc:
The self-assessment checklist is a verification list that the student themselves completes to check whether their work or performance meets the defined requirements. Each item is answered with yes/no or a check mark.

How to use it: before submitting work, the student goes through their list to make sure all required elements have been included. This reduces errors of omission and develops responsibility for one's own work.

Especially useful for tasks with clear, closed requirements (format of a report, elements of a presentation, steps of a protocol) and for review stages before the final submission.
limitations: Does not assess quality if dichotomous.

name: Improvement commitments record
summary: Document with improvement objectives and actions.
desc:
The improvement commitments record is a document where the student notes which aspects they will improve and how, after receiving feedback. It is the step that converts formative assessment into action.

How to use it: after the return of a task or a feedback session, the student writes one or several specific commitments ('I will revise the conclusion because it does not answer the initial question', 'I will cite sources in the next piece of work'). The teacher reviews these commitments on the next submission.

It closes the formative assessment cycle: gathering evidence → feedback → improvement action. Without this final step, feedback has little real impact.
limitations: Requires subsequent review.

name: Grade book
summary: Organised record of results, tasks and criteria.
desc:
The grade book is the central record where the teacher notes the grades of each student in each task, test or assessed criterion throughout the period.

How to use it: it can be physical (notebook) or digital (spreadsheet, educational platform). It includes the names of students, tasks or criteria in columns, and grades in the corresponding cells. Good organisation allows the final grade to be calculated transparently and reproducibly.

Beyond being an administrative record, it can be used to detect trends (students who consistently improve or deteriorate) and to plan personalised support.
limitations: Does not explain learning on its own.

name: Criteria grading table
summary: Table relating evidence to assessment criteria.
desc:
The criteria grading table organises the final grade broken down by assessment criteria, instead of giving a single overall mark. Each criterion has its own weight and grade.

How to use it: list each assessment criterion with its weighting and the grade obtained. The final mark results from the weighted sum. This format is required by current regulations for assessment by evaluation criteria and specific competencies.

It allows students and families to know exactly which criteria the student is performing well in and which need improvement, making the grade much more informative.
limitations: Must be well designed.

name: Weighting matrix
summary: Table of relative weights of criteria, evidence or tools.
desc:
The weighting matrix specifies what relative weight each criterion, competency or task has in the final grade. It defines the rules of the game before the assessment process begins.

How to use it: draw up a table with all assessable elements and their corresponding percentage or weight, verifying that the sum is 100%. It should be communicated to students at the start of the period so they can organise their effort in an informed way.

A good weighting matrix reflects pedagogical priorities: if the process is as important as the product, both should have a significant weight.
purpose: Calculating grades when weighting is involved.
advantages: Makes the weight of each element explicit.
limitations: Can make assessment too rigid.

name: Numerical scale
summary: Assessment expressed with numbers.
desc:
The numerical scale assigns a score on a number scale (e.g., 1–10, 0–100, 1–4) to each criterion or to the work as a whole. It is the most widespread grading system in the Spanish educational system.

How to use it: each score on the scale should have a clear meaning (what a 5 implies as opposed to a 7). Without explicit criteria, the numerical scale can be subjective and inconsistent across marking sessions. Combined with qualitative descriptors, it gains in reliability.

Its advantage is ease of communication and calculation. Its limitation is that it does not inform students of what to improve, so it must always be accompanied by qualitative feedback.
limitations: Can hide qualitative information.

name: Verbal scale
summary: Categories such as beginning, developing, adequate, advanced.
desc:
The verbal scale replaces numbers with qualitative descriptors: insufficient, sufficient, good, notable, excellent; or beginning, developing, achieved, outstanding. Each level describes a degree of achievement.

How to use it: associate each verbal level with a description of what it implies and, if necessary, an equivalent numerical score. It is the scale used by the curriculum of many educational stages.

It has the advantage of being more descriptive than a numerical grade and communicating achievement level better. It is the basis of competency achievement profiles.
limitations: Can be ambiguous without descriptors.

name: Level-to-grade converter
summary: Rule that transforms achievement levels into a grade.
desc:
The level-to-grade converter is a table or formula that translates the qualitative levels of a rubric or descriptive scale into a numerical grade, facilitating the grading process when the curriculum requires a numerical mark.

How to use it: establish the equivalence between levels and scores (e.g., 'excellent = 9–10', 'good = 7–8', 'satisfactory = 6', 'sufficient = 5', 'insufficient = 1–4'). If there are several weighted criteria, the converter calculates the final mark from the assigned levels.

It allows qualitative, learning-oriented assessment to be combined with the regulatory obligation to issue numerical grades.
purpose: Converting rubric levels or achievement levels into grades.
suitable_for: When a final grade is needed.
limitations: Must be transparent and justifiable.

name: Assessment spreadsheet
summary: Digital workbook for recording, calculating and analysing data.
desc:
The assessment spreadsheet (Excel, Google Sheets, etc.) is a digital record that centralises all grades and allows automatic calculation of averages, weightings and final marks.

How to use it: organise it with students in rows and tasks or criteria in columns. Formulas automatically calculate weighted averages. It can include progress charts, filters by student or criterion and export to reports.

It is the most efficient assessment tool for managing grading in large groups. With macros or specific add-ons, it can automatically generate personalised reports for each student.
purpose: Organising data and applying calculation rules.
suitable_for: Assessments with a large amount of data.
limitations: Can generate false precision.

name: Individual assessment report
summary: Document communicating progress, achievements and guidance.
desc:
The individual assessment report is a document that gathers, for a specific student, information about their level of achievement in the assessed criteria or competencies, together with observations on the process and recommendations for improvement.

How to produce it: integrate information from different sources (grades, observations, self-assessment, interviews). It goes beyond the report card: it includes qualitative context that explains the results and guides the next steps.

It is the most comprehensive communication document between the school and the family. Its production requires time, but has high orientating value for the student and their families.
limitations: Requires careful synthesis.

name: Assessment record
summary: Formal record of decisions and results.
desc:
The assessment record is the official document that gathers the final grades of all students in a group at the end of an assessment period (term, year). It has administrative and legal value.

How to use it: it is completed in assessment sessions, records grades by subject or criterion and the decision on promotion or qualification. It must be signed by all teachers of the group and filed according to school regulations.

It is the final record of the assessment process. Its correct completion is an administrative obligation, but the pedagogical process that underpins it is what gives it meaning.
limitations: Does not substitute pedagogical tools.

name: Digital form
summary: Form for collecting responses or assessments.
desc:
The digital form (Google Forms, Microsoft Forms, Typeform, etc.) allows responses to be collected in a structured and automatic way: self-assessment questionnaires, satisfaction surveys, closed-answer tests, exit tickets or activity records.

How to use it: design the form with closed questions (multiple choice, Likert scale, checkboxes) or open questions. Responses are automatically centralised in a spreadsheet or results dashboard. It can be configured to give immediate feedback to the student.

It saves marking time for closed assessments, facilitates analysis of group results and allows quick diagnoses to be carried out at the start of class.
limitations: Depends on question design.

name: Digital rubric
summary: Rubric integrated in a digital platform.
desc:
The digital rubric is a rubric implemented in an educational platform or application (Google Classroom, Canvas, Moodle, Rubric Maker, etc.) that allows it to be applied directly in the digital environment, automate score calculation and send feedback to the student.

How to use it: create the rubric in the platform, associating it with a task. When marking, the teacher marks the levels directly in the platform and adds comments. The score is calculated automatically and the student receives the feedback in their personal space.

It reduces marking time, facilitates consistency across marking sessions and allows the student to access their feedback immediately and in an organised way.
limitations: Can depend on the platform.

name: Revision history
summary: Record of changes in shared documents.
desc:
The revision history is the automatic record of changes generated by collaborative editing applications (Google Docs, Word Online, etc.). It allows viewing who made which changes, when and in what order throughout the entire process of creating a document.

How to use it: by opening the version history of a shared document, the teacher can see the individual contribution of each group member, detect whether the work was done sustainedly or at the last minute, and identify who contributed the key ideas.

It is very valuable process evidence in digital group work, as it makes visible what would otherwise be invisible: who worked, when and how much.
limitations: Does not always reflect quality of contribution.

name: Progress dashboard
summary: View of progress on tasks, criteria or competencies.
desc:
The progress dashboard is a visual representation of the state of progress of each student in the objectives, tasks or criteria of the course. It can be a physical board in the classroom or a digital dashboard on the educational platform.

How to use it: design it with rows of students and columns of objectives or tasks. The status of each cell (achieved, developing, pending) is updated periodically. A well-designed dashboard allows the teacher to see the state of the group at a glance and each student to know their own progress.

It promotes transparency, self-regulation and motivation. In digital versions, it can be updated automatically with platform data.
limitations: Must be interpreted with caution.

name: Learning analytics
summary: Access, participation or activity data on platforms.
desc:
Learning analytics are data automatically collected by educational platforms (LMS, applications, educational games) about students' behaviour and performance: access time, attempts, errors, navigation patterns, scores.

How to use them: the platform generates automatic reports that the teacher can review to detect who does not access the materials, which content generates more errors or which students need support. They should not be used as the sole source of assessment.

Especially useful for identifying group and individual trends efficiently. The teacher's pedagogical judgement is essential for interpreting the data and not reducing learning to metrics.
limitations: Does not directly equate to learning.

name: Digital evidence bank
summary: Digital folder or space with assessment evidence.
desc:
The digital evidence bank is an organised repository (folder in Drive, digital portfolio, specific platform) where each student's productions and assessment documents are stored over time.

How to use it: each student has their own space where they keep their work, drafts, self-assessments and received feedback. The teacher can access it to review longitudinal progress. At the end of the course, the bank provides a complete picture of the learning journey.

It is the foundation of the digital portfolio. It enables continuous assessment, reflection on progress and communication of evidence to families and other teachers.
limitations: Requires good organisation.

name: List of indicators per criterion
summary: Breakdown of a criterion into observable indicators.
desc:
The list of indicators per criterion converts a broad criterion into observable behaviours, products or features.

How to use it: write clear indicators, link them to specific evidence and check they do not reduce the criterion to irrelevant details. It can serve as a prior step to a rubric or observation guide.

It is useful for making criteria-based assessment more transparent.
purpose: Specifying broad criteria and guiding evidence collection.
advantages: Improves alignment between criterion, task and evidence.
limitations: Can fragment learning if it becomes a mechanical list.

name: Progress scale
summary: Scale describing successive advances towards a learning goal.
desc:
The progress scale describes successive states of learning, from an initial level to autonomous or advanced performance.

How to use it: define clear levels, review periodically where each student is, what evidence shows it and what the next step is.

Very useful for continuous assessment and self-regulation, because it helps understand learning as development.
purpose: Visualising progress and guiding next steps.
suitable_for: Continuous assessment, learning portfolios and long processes.
advantages: Shows development, not just the final result.
limitations: Requires periodic review of comparable evidence.

name: Observation guide
summary: Document with focus areas, indicators and guidelines for observing a performance.
desc:
The observation guide specifies what will be looked at, when and with which indicators. It can include spaces for notes, observable examples and basic achievement levels.

How to use it: before the activity, select a few important focus areas. During observation, record evidence, not general impressions.

Especially useful when several teachers are observing or when the activity is complex.
purpose: Planning and making observation more consistent.
suitable_for: Practical activities, debates, seminars, challenges and cooperative work.
limitations: Does not replace professional judgement or well-formulated criteria.

name: Feedback/feedforward template
summary: Template for indicating current achievement, necessary improvement and next step.
desc:
The feedback/feedforward template organises feedback around three questions: what is achieved, what needs to be improved and what the next step is.

How to use it: link the comment to specific criteria and leave a verifiable improvement action. It can be used by the teacher, by peers or in guided self-assessment.

Its strength lies in shifting marking from final judgement towards future improvement.
suitable_for: Drafts, partial submissions, learning portfolios and complex tasks.
advantages: Helps turn the comment into action.
limitations: Loses value if filled in with generic phrases.

name: Group contract
summary: Agreement on roles, commitments and operating norms of a team.
desc:
The group contract records roles, responsibilities, norms, timeline and commitments of team members.

How to use it: it is agreed at the start of the work and reviewed at intermediate milestones. It can be combined with peer assessment and improvement commitments records.

Useful for assessing and regulating the cooperative process more fairly.
purpose: Making responsibilities explicit and preventing cooperation problems.
suitable_for: Cooperative work, projects and collaborative documents.
limitations: Must be reviewed during the process, not remain as an initial formality.

name: Sociogram
summary: Representation of relationships, choices or dynamics within the group.
desc:
The sociogram represents relationships within the group: work preferences, support, rejections, leadership or isolation.

How to use it: information is gathered through careful questions, analysed confidentially and used to improve groupings or coexistence, not to label students.

It is an assessment tool of the social context of learning rather than of academic performance.
purpose: Understanding integration, leadership, isolation or social structure in the classroom.
suitable_for: Tutoring, coexistence, cooperative work and groupings.
advantages: Helps make grouping and coexistence decisions.
limitations: Handles sensitive information and must be used with confidentiality.

name: Evidence map by criterion
summary: Table linking criteria to tasks, evidence and assessment instruments.
desc:
The evidence map by criterion links each assessment criterion to the tasks that evidence it, the evidence/media used and the assessment instruments applied.

How to use it: it is built as a planning and monitoring table. It allows detection of over-represented criteria, criteria without evidence and poorly justified grading decisions.

It is a key assessment tool for criteria-based assessment and evidence triangulation.
purpose: Checking coverage and traceability of assessment.
suitable_for: Criteria-based assessment, competency assessment, learning portfolios and criteria-based grading.
advantages: Makes visible which evidence supports each decision.
limitations: Requires updating and careful selection of relevant evidence.

name: AI and sources use declaration
summary: Template for declaring support, sources, AI tools and personal decisions in a task.
desc:
The AI and sources use declaration is a short template that accompanies a task to explain which external supports were used and which part of the work reflects the student's own decisions.

How to use it: it can include sections on sources consulted, AI tools used, the purpose of each support, revised fragments, errors found, changes made and final responsibility for the content. It is more useful when requested together with drafts, revision history or a short defence of the work.

It does not replace a rubric or prove learning by itself, but it helps turn AI use into a transparent, reviewable and educationally useful practice.
purpose: Document authorship, AI use, source checking and responsibility for the final production.
suitable_for: Written productions, projects, portfolios, digital products and tasks completed outside the classroom.
advantages: Makes the process transparent and supports feedback conversations about authorship and work quality.
limitations: It depends on student honesty and should be combined with process review or oral defence when needed.

name: Table of specifications
summary: A matrix crossing the content to be assessed with cognitive levels and distributing the weight of each item, to design balanced, valid tests before writing them.
desc:
The table of specifications (or test blueprint) is a design tool created before writing a test. In a two-way table it crosses the content or learning to be assessed (rows) with the cognitive levels to be measured (columns, for example following Bloom’s taxonomy or depth of knowledge), and states how many items and what weight correspond to each cell.

How to use it: list the unit’s key learning, decide the intended cognitive level for each and assign the number of questions and the score in proportion to their importance. The items are then written from that matrix, ensuring the test covers the essentials and does not concentrate only on rote recall or a single topic.

Its value is to ensure content validity and a balanced test: it avoids biased exams, makes transparent what is assessed and with what weight, and connects directly with the alignment between objectives, teaching and assessment.
purpose: Design balanced, valid tests by distributing content, cognitive levels and weights.
advantages: Ensures content validity and balance; makes transparent what is assessed and with what weight.
limitations: Requires upfront planning time and command of the cognitive levels.

name: Single-point rubric
summary: A rubric that describes only the target level (the expected performance) for each criterion, leaving space to note where work falls short and where it exceeds it. It simplifies design and prioritises feedback.
desc:
The single-point rubric is a simplified variant of the analytic rubric. Instead of describing every level of performance for each criterion, it describes only the target level (what is expected) in a central column, and reserves a column on the left to note where the work falls short and another on the right to note where it exceeds expectations.

How to use it: write the criteria and, for each, only the descriptor of the expected level. When marking, the teacher (or the student or a peer) writes concrete comments in the “not yet there” and “exceeds” columns, personalising the feedback instead of ticking a box.

Its value is that it is quick to design, does not box learners into predefined levels and shifts the focus towards qualitative feedback and improvement. It is especially suitable for self- and peer assessment and for open or creative tasks.
purpose: Assess criteria by describing only the expected level and focusing marking on feedback.
advantages: Quick to design, does not box into levels and prioritises qualitative feedback.
limitations: Provides less detail than an analytic rubric and depends on the quality of written comments.

name: Ladder of feedback
summary: A structured feedback protocol in four rungs —clarify, value, ask questions and suggest— that orders comments so they are respectful, specific and useful, especially among peers.
desc:
The ladder of feedback, proposed within the Project Zero framework, is a protocol that organises feedback into a sequence of four steps so that it is constructive and does not turn into vague or hurtful criticism.

The four rungs are: 1) Clarify (ask questions to understand well what has been done, avoiding assumptions); 2) Value (point out strengths and what works specifically); 3) Ask questions (raise doubts and concerns as questions, not judgements); and 4) Suggest (offer concrete ideas for improvement).

How to use it: before a peer-review session or a discussion of a piece of work, the ladder is presented and comments are asked to follow that order. It works very well with a script or template providing sentence starters for each rung. Its value is to create a safe climate for feedback, improve the quality of comments and teach learners to give and receive feedback.
purpose: Structure feedback into steps (clarify, value, ask, suggest) so it is useful and respectful.
advantages: Creates a safe climate, improves feedback quality and teaches how to give and receive it.
limitations: Requires practice and prior modelling so it does not become a mechanical formality.

name: Self-regulation guide (base of orientation)
summary: A guide listing the key steps and criteria for correctly carrying out a task or procedure, which learners use to self-regulate while working and later to self-assess.
desc:
The base of orientation is an instrument of formative-as-learning assessment: a guide, ideally built with the learners, that sets out in order the steps, operations and criteria needed to perform a task or procedure well (solving a problem, writing an argumentative text, carrying out a lab practical).

How it is applied: after jointly analysing how a task is done well, a list or diagram is built with the key actions and the points where it is worth pausing. Learners keep it in front of them while working and use it to guide and review their own process; over time they internalise it until they no longer need it. It also works as an instrument for self-assessment and error detection.

Its value is developing autonomy and self-regulation: it hands learners control of the process, makes the “how to do it” explicit and reduces dependence on the teacher. It is especially useful for complex procedures and for attention to diversity.
purpose: Guide the execution and self-regulation of a task through its key steps and criteria.
advantages: Develops autonomy and self-regulation and makes the procedure explicit.
limitations: If handed over ready-made instead of built with learners, it may be used mechanically.
